From Wikipedia, the free encyclopedia

Film by David F. Sandberg

***Lights Out*** is a 2016 American [supernatural horror film](/wiki/Supernatural_horror_film "Supernatural horror film") directed by [David F. Sandberg](/wiki/David_F._Sandberg "David F. Sandberg") (in his [directorial debut](/wiki/List_of_directorial_debuts "List of directorial debuts")) and written by [Eric Heisserer](/wiki/Eric_Heisserer "Eric Heisserer"), who based the screenplay on Sandberg's [2013 short film of the same name](/wiki/Lights_Out_(2013_film) "Lights Out (2013 film)"). It follows a woman who must protect her young half-brother against a spirit from her family's past that kills its victims in the dark. The film stars [Teresa Palmer](/wiki/Teresa_Palmer "Teresa Palmer"), [Gabriel Bateman](/wiki/Gabriel_Bateman "Gabriel Bateman"), [Alexander DiPersia](/wiki/Alexander_DiPersia "Alexander DiPersia"), [Billy Burke](/wiki/Billy_Burke_(actor) "Billy Burke (actor)"), and [Maria Bello](/wiki/Maria_Bello "Maria Bello"). It also features Sandberg's wife [Lotta Losten](/wiki/Lotta_Losten "Lotta Losten"), who starred in the short film.[[4]](#cite_note-4)

Produced by Heisserer, [Lawrence Grey](/wiki/Lawrence_Grey_(producer) "Lawrence Grey (producer)"), and [James Wan](/wiki/James_Wan "James Wan"), the film was shot in [Los Angeles](/wiki/Los_Angeles "Los Angeles") from June to August 2015. It had its world premiere at the [Los Angeles Film Festival](/wiki/Los_Angeles_Film_Festival "Los Angeles Film Festival") on June 8, 2016, and was released in the United States and Canada by [Warner Bros. Pictures](/wiki/Warner_Bros._Pictures "Warner Bros. Pictures") on July 22, 2016. The film grossed $148 million against a budget of $4.9 million and received mixed reviews from critics. A sequel is in development.

## Plot

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Lights_Out_(2016_film)&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: Plot")]

In a textile factory during closing hours, after an employee named Esther says goodbye to her boss Paul, she encounters a silhouette of a strange looking [witch](/wiki/Witchcraft "Witchcraft") with claw-like monstery hands when the lights are off, but she cannot see it when the lights are on. After she leaves, Paul encounters the woman as well. He tries to run away and locks himself into an office, but is killed after the lights go out.

Some time later Paul's son, Martin, witnesses his mother, Sophie, talking to something in the dark. Sophie notices Martin and comforts him about the event, but as Martin goes back to bed he notices a strange figure in the shadow behind Sophie, and locks himself in his bedroom out of fear. Shortly thereafter Martin begins to have trouble staying awake at school. The nurse is unable to get in touch with Sophie to inform her of this, so Martin's older maternal half-sister (Paul's step-daughter) Rebecca is called in to the school nurse's office instead. A [Child Protective Services](/wiki/Child_Protective_Services "Child Protective Services") agent questions Rebecca about Martin's living conditions, and Rebecca tells the official that Sophie is taking [antidepressants](/wiki/Antidepressants "Antidepressants").

Martin tells her that their mother has been talking to a woman named "Diana". Rebecca assures him that Diana is not real, and that she had also heard their mother talk to the [imaginary girl](/wiki/Imaginary_friend "Imaginary friend") when she was a child. Once they arrive at Martin's house, Rebecca gets into an argument with Sophie when she realizes her mother is not taking her medication, but Sophie tells Rebecca she has no right to lecture her, accusing Rebecca of abandoning her like Rebecca's father did. Questioning her mother's sanity, Rebecca takes Martin to her apartment, much to Sophie's despair. That night, Rebecca is awoken by the same shadowed woman that killed Paul. She narrowly escapes an attack when she turns the lights on, making the woman disappear. The next morning, Rebecca notices that the name "Diana" has been scratched into her floor, along with a scratch drawing of a stick figure. She remembers finding the same name and drawing as a child and realizes Diana is real.

Rebecca finds a box of medical records and research in Paul's office, which reveal that Sophie was admitted to a mental hospital as a child. While there, she befriended a young patient named Diana, who suffered from a severe skin condition that meant she could not go out in sunlight. It was believed that she was evil and was able to "get into people's heads" since Diana's father committed suicide after Diana influenced him to do so. In the end, Diana was accidentally reduced to ashes by the hospital staff when they tried to perform an experimental treatment on her under intense light. Sophie has a conversation with Martin, who was taken back to their mother by the CPS agent, and she decides to allow Martin to meet Diana after turning the light off. However, Martin is frightened by Diana and runs away from them back to Rebecca's place.

Rebecca, her boyfriend Bret, and Martin stage an intervention with Sophie about how she is letting Diana haunt them. Sophie becomes irate but secretly asks Rebecca for help, as Diana will not let Sophie go. The group stays the night at Sophie's, intending to get her help in the morning. To avoid an attack from Diana during the night, they rig the house to be as brightly lit as possible, but this proves useless when Diana cuts the power of the whole neighborhood. She traps Rebecca and Martin in the basement and attempts to kill Bret. Despite being injured, Bret escapes by shining the headlight of his car and drives away. Later, Sophie confronts Diana, trying to tell her to spare her children, but is knocked unconscious by Diana when she tries to take her medicine. In the basement, Martin and Rebecca find a black-light; Rebecca is able to clearly see Diana with it, but realizes it is not powerful enough to ward her off.

Bret returns with two police officers. When the officers free Martin and Rebecca from the basement, they also see the shadow of Diana and try to go after her, but she kills them. Rebecca and Martin exit the house, but Martin insists on getting Sophie out as well. Rebecca goes back alone for her mother. As she climbs the stairs, Diana reveals that she killed Rebecca's father years ago because, like Paul, he tried to help Sophie recover from her trauma. She injures Rebecca for trying to do the same, but as Diana prepares to kill her, Sophie, who has recovered, appears with a gun from one of the dead officers and shoots at Diana, who says that can't harm her, to which Sophie replies, "This will. There's no you without me." Sophie has realized she is the tether for Diana to exist in the real world and kills herself to save Rebecca and Martin. As Sophie's body falls, Diana vanishes from existence.

After the long night, Bret comforts Martin and Rebecca in an ambulance outside the house; a light flickers inside the ambulance but Bret assures them that Diana is truly gone.

## Cast

* [Alicia Vela-Bailey](/wiki/Alicia_Vela-Bailey "Alicia Vela-Bailey") as Diana, a [malevolent spirit](/wiki/Malevolent_spirit "Malevolent spirit") and Sophie's friend
  + [Ava Cantrell](/wiki/Ava_Cantrell "Ava Cantrell") as teen Diana
* [Andi Osho](/wiki/Andi_Osho "Andi Osho") as Emma, a social worker
* Rolando Boyce as Brian Andrews
* Maria Russell as Officer Gomez
* Elizabeth Pan as Nurse
* [Lotta Losten](/wiki/Lotta_Losten "Lotta Losten") as Esther, Paul's assistant

## Production

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Lights_Out_(2016_film)&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Production")]

### Development

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Lights_Out_(2016_film)&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: Development")]

Sandberg, along with his wife Lotta Losten, created the initial short film for a film competition. Although the film did not win the competition, the short soon went viral, leading to Sandberg being contacted by several agents, to the point where he had to develop a [spreadsheet](/wiki/Spreadsheet "Spreadsheet") to keep track of them all.[[5]](#cite_note-Airbnbdaily-5) One of the contacts was Lawrence Grey who wanted to collaborate with [James Wan](/wiki/James_Wan "James Wan") in order to produce a feature-length version. Although Wan enjoyed the short, he was hesitant that it could be turned into a feature until Sandberg produced a treatment for the feature-length version.[[5]](#cite_note-Airbnbdaily-5)

The move to [Hollywood](/wiki/Hollywood,_Los_Angeles "Hollywood, Los Angeles") was somewhat hectic for the couple, requiring that Losten quit her day job in order to do so. Once in Hollywood the two were unable to get an apartment due to not having a US credit score, forcing them to rent [Airbnb](/wiki/Airbnb "Airbnb") on a monthly basis.[[5]](#cite_note-Airbnbdaily-5)

Sandberg originally based the character of Rebecca on a real girl that he knew who was suffering from depression, and who was engaging in [self-harm](/wiki/Self-harm "Self-harm"), which is why Rebecca has scars on her arms, but the development of the film made it less about depression and more of a ghost story in which Diana would have been the real person who died and became a ghost. Wan came up with the idea of making Diana the ghost. Rebecca's boyfriend was also given a twist of being a rocker, but is actually committed and responsible, even driving a safe car like a Volvo. Another twist Sandberg liked was making the imaginary friend for the mother rather than the trope of having the friend be for the child.[[6]](#cite_note-av_club-6)

### Casting

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Lights_Out_(2016_film)&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: Casting")]

In June 2015, [Gabriel Bateman](/wiki/Gabriel_Bateman "Gabriel Bateman") and [Teresa Palmer](/wiki/Teresa_Palmer "Teresa Palmer") were cast in the film as the child and teenager leads. In that same month, [Maria Bello](/wiki/Maria_Bello "Maria Bello") was cast in the film as the mother of Bateman and Palmer's characters, alongside [Alexander DiPersia](/wiki/Alexander_DiPersia "Alexander DiPersia") as the boyfriend of Palmer's character, [Billy Burke](/wiki/Billy_Burke_(actor) "Billy Burke (actor)") as the stepfather of Palmer's character and father of Bateman's character, and [Alicia Vela-Bailey](/wiki/Alicia_Vela-Bailey "Alicia Vela-Bailey") as the main antagonist [Diana](/wiki/Diana_(Lights_Out) "Diana (Lights Out)") were also starring.[[7]](#cite_note-7)[[8]](#cite_note-8)[[9]](#cite_note-9)[[10]](#cite_note-June2015HellHorror-10)

### Filming

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Lights_Out_(2016_film)&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: Filming")]

Principal photography for the film began in June 2015 in [Los Angeles](/wiki/Los_Angeles "Los Angeles"). Filming was completed on August 5, 2015.[[11]](#cite_note-James_Wan_on_Instagram-11) Sandberg had not worked with a [film crew](/wiki/Film_crew "Film crew") or visited a [film set](/wiki/Film_set "Film set") before directing *Lights Out*; he had to ask the [first assistant director](/wiki/First_assistant_director "First assistant director"), "So when do I say action?"[[12]](#cite_note-sandberg20160809-12)

### Special effects

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Lights_Out_(2016_film)&action=edit&section=7 "Edit section: Special effects")]

Special effects of having the ghost appear and disappear were mostly done by using a [split-screen](/wiki/Split_screen_(video_production) "Split screen (video production)") technique as also used in the short. Sandberg said "Whenever she's in frame with another character, it's basically just a split screen. So you shoot it with her and without her. You turn the camera on with her, you turn it off and she walks off, and then you turn it on again. It's super simple, actually." Sandberg also made a list of what he called the "light gags", or different ways to create light sources from flashlights to cell phones and gunfire. In the scene when Diana appears in Rebecca's room, James Wan suggested replacing passing car headlights in an early treatment with the flashing neon sign that appears in the final film.[[6]](#cite_note-av_club-6)

### Ending Concerns

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Lights_Out_(2016_film)&action=edit&section=8 "Edit section: Ending Concerns")]

After hearing concerns from critics that the ending of the film was promoting suicide, Sandberg interviewed with *[The A.V. Club](/wiki/The_A.V._Club "The A.V. Club")* where he said he was distressed about that idea, and wanted to explain his position. He said that he originally wanted to make a film about depression, as he has also suffered from it, and that one of his friends had died by suicide. Diana was not a ghost back then, but during the development of the film, it became more of a horror film. It still retained some themes about depression and mental illness. He had originally shot a second ending to the film in which Martin becomes depressed and Diana comes back one more time before she is killed. Test audiences concluded that Sophie's sacrifice would have been in vain.[[6]](#cite_note-av_club-6)

## Release

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Lights_Out_(2016_film)&action=edit&section=9 "Edit section: Release")]

The film had its world premiere at the [Los Angeles Film Festival](/wiki/Los_Angeles_Film_Festival "Los Angeles Film Festival") on June 8, 2016.[[13]](#cite_note-13) The film also screened at [Comic-Con](/wiki/New_York_Comic_Con "New York Comic Con") on July 20, 2016,[[14]](#cite_note-14) and was released on July 22, 2016.[[15]](#cite_note-15)

## Reception

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Lights_Out_(2016_film)&action=edit&section=10 "Edit section: Reception")]

### Box office

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Lights_Out_(2016_film)&action=edit&section=11 "Edit section: Box office")]

*Lights Out* grossed $67.3 million in the United States and Canada and $81.6 million in other territories for a worldwide total of $148.9 million, against a production budget of $4.9 million.[[3]](#cite_note-BOM-3)

In North America, *Lights Out* was projected to gross $13–15 million from 2,900 theaters in its opening weekend.[[16]](#cite_note-preview-16) It made $1.8 million from its Thursday night screenings and $9.2 million on its first day.[[17]](#cite_note-17) The film exceeded expectations and earned $21.7 million in its opening weekend, finishing at third place behind fellow newcomer *[Star Trek Beyond](/wiki/Star_Trek_Beyond "Star Trek Beyond")* and holdover *[The Secret Life of Pets](/wiki/The_Secret_Life_of_Pets "The Secret Life of Pets")*.[[18]](#cite_note-18)[[19]](#cite_note-19)

In other territories, the film earned $8.5 million in its opening weekend from 3,737 screens in key markets of [Russia](/wiki/Russia "Russia") and [Australia](/wiki/Australia "Australia") along with 30 smaller [Eastern European](/wiki/Eastern_Europe "Eastern Europe") and [Asian](/wiki/Asia "Asia") markets. The film benefited from being released in the wake of the global success of *[The Conjuring 2](/wiki/The_Conjuring_2 "The Conjuring 2")*.[[20]](#cite_note-Overseas30thOpening-20) It debuted at first place in Russia with $1.7 million.[[20]](#cite_note-Overseas30thOpening-20) Its other top openings were recorded in South Korea ($3.9 million), France ($1.5 million), the U.K. ($1.4 million) and Spain ($1.1 million).[[21]](#cite_note-Overseas36thOpening-21)[[22]](#cite_note-Overseas37thOpening-22) Its biggest earning markets are South Korea ($7.7 million), Mexico ($5.5 million), the U.K. ($4.5 million) and Spain ($3.9 million).[[23]](#cite_note-Overseas39thOpening-23)

### Critical response

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Lights_Out_(2016_film)&action=edit&section=12 "Edit section: Critical response")]

On [review aggregator](/wiki/Review_aggregator "Review aggregator") [Rotten Tomatoes](/wiki/Rotten_Tomatoes "Rotten Tomatoes") the film has an approval rating of 75% based on 183 reviews, with a [weighted average](/wiki/Weighted_average "Weighted average") rating of 6.3/10. The site's critical consensus reads, "*Lights Out* makes skillful use of sturdy genre tropes—and some terrific performances—for an unsettling, fright-filled experience that delivers superior chills without skimping on story."[[24]](#cite_note-RT-24) On [Metacritic](/wiki/Metacritic "Metacritic"), the film has a score of 58 out of 100, based on 34 critics, indicating "mixed or average" reviews.[[25]](#cite_note-Meta-25) Audiences polled by [CinemaScore](/wiki/CinemaScore "CinemaScore") gave the film an average grade of "B" on an A+ to F scale.[[26]](#cite_note-CinemaScore-26)

Lucy O'Brien of *[IGN](/wiki/IGN "IGN")* gave the film 7/10, saying: "[w]ith an unnerving monster at its core, great cast and relentless final sequence, *Light's [*[sic](/wiki/Sic "Sic")*] Out* is a debut director Sandberg should be proud of. A clunky script occasionally loosens its grip on the nerves, but chances are Diana will still have you sleeping with the lights on for a good while after leaving the theatre."[[27]](#cite_note-27) [Richard Roeper](/wiki/Richard_Roeper "Richard Roeper") of the *[Chicago Sun-Times](/wiki/Chicago_Sun-Times "Chicago Sun-Times")* awarded the film 4 stars out of 4, stating: "[e]ven the most cynical, jaded, seen-it-all-before critic cannot deny certain visceral reactions to a film. *Lights Out* gave me the chills."[[28]](#cite_note-28) Justin Lowe of *[The Hollywood Reporter](/wiki/The_Hollywood_Reporter "The Hollywood Reporter")* wrote, "[a] surprisingly [maternal](/wiki/Maternal "Maternal") horror movie that relies as much on fraying emotional bonds as supernatural suspense to create tension, *Lights Out* deals with an array of primal fears that threaten to unravel a family's fundamental relationships, along with their sanity."[[29]](#cite_note-29) Jeannette Catsoulis of *[The New York Times](/wiki/The_New_York_Times "The New York Times")* wrote, "[s]packling over any copycat cracks with strong acting and fleet editing, *Lights Out* delivers minimalist frights in old-school ways."[[30]](#cite_note-30)

A few critics were less taken with the film. [James Berardinelli](/wiki/James_Berardinelli "James Berardinelli") of *Reelviews* gave 2 stars out of 4, saying: "[u]nfortunately, the film stumbles, offering too few legitimate scares and displaying an overreliance on traditional horror movie clichés." Berardinelli detested the film's camerawork, described characters as being "thinly drawn", and the screenplay as "spending inordinate amount of time providing a backstory..."[[31]](#cite_note-31) [Rex Reed](/wiki/Rex_Reed "Rex Reed") of *[Observer](/wiki/Observer.com "Observer.com")* gave 1 star out of 4, saying: "the film's screenplay focuses almost entirely on the number of resourceful and ingenious ways the characters dream up to keep the lights on, stave off the next attack and stay alive—lights from candles, flashlights, cellphones, the car in the driveway—before the batteries die; The fun wears out fast and so does the "gotcha" factor."[[32]](#cite_note-32)

## Sequel

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Lights_Out_(2016_film)&action=edit&section=14 "Edit section: Sequel")]

In July 2016, a week after the film's release, it was announced that [New Line Cinema](/wiki/New_Line_Cinema "New Line Cinema") and [Warner Bros. Pictures](/wiki/Warner_Bros._Pictures "Warner Bros. Pictures") had greenlit a sequel. Heisserer and Sandberg will return to write and direct the film, respectively, while Wan and Lawrence Grey will return to produce under their [Atomic Monster](/wiki/Atomic_Monster "Atomic Monster") and Grey Matter Productions banners.[[33]](#cite_note-33)[[34]](#cite_note-34)

As of 2026, there has been no update on the sequel's development.[[35]](#cite_note-35)
